dateStatements.label: Current specialist dating
dateStatements.notes: DMR gives the 1420s; Ackerman reports an argument for approximately 1425 tied to the death of Lovelich's patron Henry Barton in 1434.

dateStatements.label: Traditional dating
dateStatements.notes: A competing traditional work date, preserved separately from both the 1420s claim and the later physical-witness date.

entities.description: Herry Lovelich's Middle English verse translation and adaptation of the French Vulgate Merlin, probably composed in London in the 1420s according to the Database of Middle English Romance. The unique manuscript preserves 27,852 lines in rhyming couplets but loses an unknown number of leaves at the end; the extant translation stops about halfway through its French source, and it is unknown whether Lovelich translated more.
entities.label: Merlin
notes: Extant records that the work survives; the unique witness is end-defective, and Kock's transcription is complete only relative to the surviving text.
Herry is retained as the preferred given-name form used by the edition and Ackerman; Henry is a common modern authority form.

rights.basis: The medieval work predates modern copyright by centuries. Physical ownership, modern editions, encodings, and manuscript-image rights remain separate.

entities.description: The French prose Arthurian romance translated and versified by Lovelich. Current scholarship identifies the Vulgate Merlin as his textual source tradition, but no surviving physical French manuscript is established as his actual exemplar.
entities.label: French Vulgate Merlin

bibliographic.citation: Herry Lovelich, Merlin: A Middle-English Metrical Version of a French Romance, ed. Ernst A. Kock, Part I, Early English Text Society, Extra Series 93 (London: Kegan Paul, Trench, Trubner & Co., 1904), pp. 1-208, lines 1-7,868.
bibliographic.title: Merlin: A Middle-English Metrical Version of a French Romance

entities.description: The first installment of Kock's transcription of the unique manuscript: editorial lines 1-7,868, printed pp. 1-208, with chapter headings, marginal summaries, manuscript-column milestones, italic expansions, and textual footnotes.
entities.label: Ernst A. Kock, Merlin, Part I (1904)

rights.basis: The installment was published more than 95 years ago; Internet Archive metadata for the combined scan also records possible-copyright-status NOT_IN_COPYRIGHT in the United States.
rights.component: 1904 Part I text, typography, and apparatus
notes: Status outside the United States was not separately assessed.

bibliographic.citation: Herry Lovelich, Merlin: A Middle-English Metrical Version of a French Romance, ed. Ernst A. Kock, Part II, Early English Text Society, Extra Series 112 (London: Early English Text Society, 1913), pp. 209-416, lines 7,869-15,556.
bibliographic.title: Merlin: A Middle-English Metrical Version of a French Romance

entities.description: The second installment of Kock's transcription: editorial lines 7,869-15,556, printed pp. 209-416. CME00134 includes its entire text but not its title page and preliminary matter.
entities.label: Ernst A. Kock, Merlin, Part II (1913)

rights.basis: The installment was published more than 95 years ago; Internet Archive metadata for the combined scan also records possible-copyright-status NOT_IN_COPYRIGHT in the United States.
rights.component: 1913 Part II text, typography, and apparatus
notes: Status outside the United States was not separately assessed.

bibliographic.citation: Herry Lovelich, Merlin: A Middle-English Metrical Version of a French Romance, ed. Ernst A. Kock, Part III, Early English Text Society, Original Series 185 (London: Oxford University Press for the Early English Text Society, 1932), pp. 417-739, lines 15,557-27,852.
bibliographic.title: Merlin: A Middle-English Metrical Version of a French Romance

entities.description: The final textual installment of Kock's edition, continuing the extant manuscript from line 15,557 through line 27,852 on printed pp. 417-739. It completes the printed surviving text, not the lost ending of the medieval romance. Ackerman reports that the promised broader critical apparatus was not completed.
entities.label: Ernst A. Kock, Merlin, Part III (1932)

rights.basis: A 1932 publication can remain within the maximum United States term through the end of 2027 if statutory copyright was secured and renewed; no renewal determination was completed.
notes: The underlying medieval text remains public domain; this status concerns Kock's 1932 editorial layer.

dateStatements.label: James catalogue dating
dateStatements.notes: James's broad date for the physical manuscript; it is not a date for Lovelich's composition event.

dateStatements.label: Parker summary dating
dateStatements.notes: Parker's narrower summary date for the physical witness, retained separately from James's broader fifteenth-century dating.

entities.description: The unique manuscript of Lovelich's History of the Holy Grail and Merlin, produced in England in the fifteenth century. Parker associates Lovelich and his work with London's Skinners' Company, but that authorial context does not establish London as the manuscript's copying place. Parker broadly catalogs Merlin at ff. 88v-200v and says it ends imperfectly. James's collation shows that current ff. 197-198 are displaced Holy Grail leaves, while ff. 199-200 are the two surviving leaves of quire P; the surviving Merlin text is therefore represented in current order at ff. 88v-196v and ff. 199r-200v.

holding.notes: Held by the Parker Library, Corpus Christi College, Cambridge.

entities.label: Cambridge, Corpus Christi College, Parker Library MS 80

physicalDescription.format: Bound codex; double columns in a current hand
notes: Double columns have approximately 65, 69, or 74 lines.
Collation: A gone; B16 wants 1-9, with B10-11 now ff.197-198 and B12-16 in situ; D16 wants 4; E16-O16; displaced B10-11; P with only leaves 1-2 surviving.
The History of the Holy Grail ends on f.88v, where Merlin begins lower on the same surface.
Current ff.197-198 preserve displaced opening Holy Grail material, not Merlin; Merlin resumes on current f.199r and ends imperfectly on f.200v.
Later fifteenth-century annotations are associated with John Cok of St Bartholomew's, Smithfield.
physicalDescription.support: Conflicting official descriptions: James says paper with twelfth-century vellum guards; Parker MODS includes both 'Paper and Vellum' and 'Vellum' material fields

rights.basis: The manuscript text is centuries outside copyright term; this does not govern Parker's modern photographs or physical-access conditions.

next_steps: Consult modern textual studies and direct collations of all distinctive Lovelich readings against French Vulgate Merlin witnesses.
Do not promote a surviving French codex into the primary chain without verbal and codicological evidence.
Retain the current work-level version_of relation until an actual physical exemplar is demonstrated.
openQuestions.question: Which physical French manuscript or textual subfamily supplied Lovelich's Vulgate Merlin exemplar?

next_steps: Obtain unrestricted access to Part III or inspect a physical copy and record every manuscript-column milestone from line 15,557 onward.
Collate the final printed lines directly against Parker canvases 399-408.
Reconcile DIMEV's ff.88v-196v endpoint with Parker's f.200v component endpoint without treating displaced ff.197-198 as Merlin.
openQuestions.question: How do Kock Part III's final lines map exactly across current ff.196v, the displaced Holy Grail leaves ff.197-198, and the surviving quire-P leaves ff.199-200?

next_steps: Ask the Parker Library whether the vellum-only field is a legacy metadata artifact or identifies a component not explained in the summary.
Inspect high-resolution surfaces and current codicological scholarship before replacing the conflict with a normalized support statement.
Continue to cite James's paper-with-vellum-guards statement separately from the MODS fields.
openQuestions.question: Why does Parker's MODS record contain both 'Paper and Vellum' and 'Vellum' material fields when James describes a paper codex with vellum guards?

next_steps: Consult EETS and Kock archival records, correspondence, proofs, and editorial papers.
Distinguish the line-level footnotes and marginal summaries printed in Parts I-II from the broader apparatus Ackerman says was lacking.
Record any surviving materials as separate archival entities rather than treating the 1932 text installment as a complete critical edition.
openQuestions.question: What unpublished or archival material survives from Kock's promised introduction, notes, glossaries, and other critical apparatus?

next_steps: Review current evidence for Henry Barton's patronage and death date, Lovelich's civic records, and linguistic chronology.
Keep work composition, translator activity, scribal copying, and manuscript production as separate dated events.
Do not transfer the witness's date or Essex scribal profile to the work without independent evidence.
openQuestions.question: Can the 1420s London composition claim be narrowed or reconciled more securely with the traditional circa-1450 date and Parker's circa-1450-75 witness date?

next_steps: Search renewal records and EETS/Oxford rights files for Original Series 185.
Use a library copy or the Internet Archive controlled-lending item for private comparison while respecting access terms.
Recheck public-domain status after 1 January 2028 if no earlier basis is established, and cache a complete authorized public source if one appears.
openQuestions.question: Can an unrestricted lawful reproduction of Kock Part III be obtained, and was its 1932 United States copyright secured and renewed?

relations.subjectLabel: Database of Middle English Romance: Merlin

reviewNotes: The intellectual work is Lovelich's Middle English Merlin; CME00134 is a partial digital representation of its opening 15,556 lines, not a complete-work encoding.

The XML reproduces a Part I title page dated 1904 but continues through all of Part II, including printed pp. 209-416 and lines 7,869-15,556; the Part II title page and preliminary matter are not transcribed.

The preferred University of Michigan/Google Internet Archive scan contains both source parts and printed p. 209. The alternate Harvard/Google scan is a complete provider object but omits printed p. 209 and therefore lacks lines 7,869-7,907.

Parker catalogs Merlin broadly at ff. 88v-200v. James's collation identifies current ff. 197-198 as displaced Holy Grail leaves originally belonging after lost leaves at the beginning; current ff. 199-200 are the two surviving leaves of quire P. The target text is therefore modeled as a discontiguous current-foliation run while Parker's own broad label is retained.

DIMEV gives the unique witness as ff. 88v-196v even while recording all 27,852 lines. This conflicts with Parker's component ending at f. 200v and with James's collation; the discrepancy remains explicit.

The Parker MODS record has internally conflicting material fields ('Paper and Vellum' and 'Vellum'), while its linked James catalogue record says paper with vellum guards. No unsupported single-support normalization is made.

The full manuscript object, not a crop of the Merlin leaves, is retained in the Git-ignored source cache. The work mapping points from the complete 414-canvas bundle to the CME selection at canvases 184-300.

The 15,556 body L elements match the terminal editorial number, but seven explicit L/@N values differ from their ordinal XML positions. Counts are encoding metrics rather than a silent reconstruction of Kock's lineation.

Kölbing's 1890 lines 1-1,638 and Furnivall's 1863 146-line Mordred excerpt are independently transcribed partial editions from the same witness. Neither is asserted as an immediate source of Kock's edition.

The Parker images are CC BY-NC 4.0. Their private local cache does not authorize commercial redistribution; Parker directs scholarly or commercial high-resolution publication enquiries to parker-library@corpus.cam.ac.uk.

lineage.summary: The checked-in XML is a repository copy of University of Michigan item CME00134. It encodes the first 15,556 lines of Herry Lovelich's Middle English verse Merlin from Ernst A. Kock's Parts I and II, published in 1904 and 1913 as EETS Extra Series 93 and 112. Although the XML title page names only Part I, its body continues through printed p. 416 and silently crosses into Part II at line 7,869. Kock transcribed the unique witness, Cambridge, Corpus Christi College, Parker Library MS 80. The encoded selection begins on f. 88v and ends within f. 146v; it does not end the medieval work. Kock's Part III (1932, EETS Original Series 185) continues through line 27,852. Parker's complete 414-canvas facsimile is cached: current ff. 197-198 are displaced leaves of the preceding Holy Grail, so the surviving Merlin text is physically represented by ff. 88v-196v and ff. 199r-200v within Parker's broader catalog component ff. 88v-200v. The manuscript and the romance both end imperfectly.

catalogingSubject.label: Merlin
catalogingSubject.scope_note: Herry Lovelich's single Middle English verse romance Merlin as preserved in its unique, end-defective witness: 27,852 extant lines. CME00134 represents only lines 1-15,556 from Ernst A. Kock's Parts I-II, not the complete surviving work. The medieval work, its French source tradition, manuscript witness, Kock's three publication installments, University of Michigan encoding, repository XML, and digital facsimiles remain distinct descriptive and lineage layers.

notes: Part I appeared in 1904, Part II in 1913, and Part III in 1932. Completion of the surviving text does not restore the manuscript's lost ending or the edition's promised critical apparatus.

extent.description: One verse work of 27,852 surviving lines in rhyming couplets. Its unique witness is end-defective, so the lost English ending and intended total extent are unknown. CME00134 contains 15,556 nonempty L elements (lines 1-15,556), leaving 12,296 extant lines outside the encoding. Its 268 LG elements and fourteen DIV2 chapters are encoding/editorial metrics, not a complete stanza count or evidence of medieval sectioning; seven explicit L/@N values diverge from ordinal XML position.

formStatements.description: 27,852 extant Middle English lines in rhyming couplets; DMR summarizes the rhyme as aa4bb4 and notes metrical variation.

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: French is the language of the source work, not an additional primary language of Lovelich's romance. Modern English, German, and French editorial matter in source editions is likewise not cataloged as language of the work.
The work-level record describes all 27,852 surviving lines. CME00134 is an incomplete digital representation of that work, even though it completely encodes the textual portions of Kock Parts I-II.

The manuscript is extant but end-defective. Kock Part III completes the edition of what survives; it neither reconstructs the lost English ending nor proves that Lovelich stopped at the surviving endpoint.

The XML has two top-level DIV1 wrappers: (//DIV1)[1] title-page paratext and (//DIV1)[2] the poem. Verse and chapter metrics must target the second wrapper; its fourteen DIV2 units are editorial chapters.
The final encoded line, 'j schal ȝow tellen, with-owten ony lette.', is not an explicit. It is followed by 12,296 surviving lines outside CME00134.

Parker's complete manuscript facsimile is locally cached in a Git-ignored directory under CC BY-NC 4.0 terms. Commercial reuse of Parker images requires attention to provider licensing and, where appropriate, direct permission; those rights attach to the images, not to the medieval text's descriptive metadata.
The exact French source work is established at the textual-tradition level, but no source-manuscript witness is invented.

next_steps: Consult textual studies comparing Lovelich's readings with Vulgate Merlin manuscript families.
Record a physical French witness only when an edition or study establishes the relationship rather than inferring it from the work-level source.
openQuestions.question: Which physical French manuscript or textual subfamily supplied Lovelich's Vulgate Merlin exemplar?

next_steps: Search for lost-fragment reports, later copies, citations, and textual references to a fuller Lovelich Merlin.
Keep the 27,852-line extent labeled extant rather than complete or authorially final.
openQuestions.question: Did Lovelich translate beyond the point where the unique manuscript now ends, and what was the original English ending?

next_steps: Review the full arguments behind DMR's dating and localization.
Separate patronage biography, author residence, composition location, copying date, and dialect localization in any refinement.
openQuestions.question: How securely can the 1420s and London composition claims be distinguished from the traditional c.1450 date and the manuscript's Essex dialect profile?

next_steps: Inspect a complete lawful copy of Kock Part III against Parker canvases 301-408.
Map each folio-column milestone and verify the transition from current f.196v to f.199r without treating displaced Holy Grail leaves as Merlin.
openQuestions.question: What is the exact Kock Part III line-to-folio and facsimile-canvas crosswalk after f.146v, especially across the displaced current ff.197-198?

notes: DMR assigns London as the place of composition. Parker links Lovelich to the London Skinners' Company; this contextual association is not itself proof of where every line was composed.
DIMEV associates manuscript CamCCC80 with LALME/eLALME linguistic profile 6360 and Essex. This is a witness-dialect statement, not a competing claim that Lovelich composed the work in Essex.

summaries.value: Merlin is Herry Lovelich's long Middle English verse translation and adaptation of the French prose Vulgate Merlin. Its 27,852 surviving lines narrate Merlin's demonic conception and Christian rescue, his prophetic and political service, Uther's reign and Arthur's conception, Arthur's accession and wars, the establishment of the Round Table, the betrothal to Guinevere, and later Arthurian campaigns and relationships. The unique manuscript ends imperfectly about halfway through the extant French source tradition, so neither the original English ending nor the extent Lovelich intended can be recovered from the sole witness.

The narrative covers Merlin's origins and education; Vortiger and the tower; Uther Pendragon; Arthur's conception, fosterage, accession, and battles; baronial and foreign conflict; the Round Table; Arthur's marriage to Guinevere or Gonnore; Gawain and other princes; Merlin's dealings with Nimiane; and the wars of Ban of Benoic and allied rulers. Themes indexed by DMR include disguise, exile, incest, marriage, military combat, siege, treachery, supernatural intervention, penance, pilgrimage, sacrament, merchants, and travel.

CME00134 encodes only Kock's lines 1-15,556, corresponding to all of Parts I-II and manuscript f.88v through part of f.146v. It stops mid-work and mid-sentence; 12,296 further extant lines appear in Kock Part III. In the XML, the first top-level DIV1 is title-page paratext and the second top-level DIV1 is the poem; fourteen DIV2 children within the poem wrapper are Kock's editorial chapters, not medieval authorial sections.

The preferred title, authorship, date, place, form, genre, and subjects describe Lovelich's work. Manuscript date and dialect, editorial chaptering and expansion, scan coverage, digital encoding structure, and provider rights are attached only to their appropriate witness, edition, or representation layers through lineage and scoped statements.

scope.description: Modern title of Kock's three-part edition, inherited by CME's encoded title page
